- Small accidental thumb movements do not steer the car.
- Medium steering corrections are easier to hold than today.
- Full lock remains reachable.
- Steering output is deterministic and unit-tested.
- Existing touch controls and tutorial selectors keep working.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/control-mapper.test.js tests/unit/joystick-shaping.test.js` covers dead zone, snap-to-zero, response curve, filtering bounds, full-lock reachability, touch cancel, and deterministic output.
- Browser-light/E2E smoke: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/player-hud-state.spec.ts` or a focused mobile-context spec proves touch input reaches shaped controls and multitouch pedals/fire coexist.
- Tuning evidence: record chosen dead zone, curve, snap threshold, and filter lag; defaults stay within the target range unless a playtest note explains the change.
- Usability guard: Local blind-operable left-half steering remains available; no tiny phone target becomes required.

title: Make phone throttle and wheelie intent controllable, not accidentally binary
description:
Track and fix the input/handling mismatch where the phone accelerator is binary but wheelie activation expects controllable throttle.

Background:
The current phone controller sends `acceleration` as 0 or 1 from the up pedal. The wheelie config activates above `0.7`, so every normal phone accelerator press can immediately become wheelie intent. This collides with the design note that players should be able to feather throttle to avoid losing steering in technical corners. Older wheelie beads implemented steering loss and payoff, but this specific binary-throttle mismatch is not tracked in open work.

Scope:
- Add throttle shaping in the shared input mapper: button-held target remains simple, but actual acceleration ramps up/down over short, tunable windows.
- Require wheelie lift to be more intentional than any instant accelerator press: e.g. shaped throttle near full for a short duration, boost/stunt intent, or another explicit condition.
- Keep beginner controls simple: hold up to go, hold down to brake/reverse. Do not require an analog throttle widget unless playtesting proves it necessary.
- Make tuning config-driven and visible in F2/debug surfaces where practical.
- Ensure phones release shaped throttle immediately enough on visibility loss or touch cancel that cars do not drive themselves.

Acceptance criteria:
- A player can pulse/feather the accelerator enough to steer through normal corners without instantly entering wheelie steering-loss behavior.
- Holding full throttle still reliably produces wheelies when the car/track should allow it.
- Wheelie entry feels intentional and explainable, not like input lag.
- Unit tests cover throttle ramping, release behavior, and wheelie activation gating.
- Existing boost/landing payoff remains intact.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/control-mapper.test.js tests/unit/wheelie-intent.test.js` covers throttle ramp up/down, immediate release on touch cancel/visibility loss, and wheelie activation gating.
- Integration/sim proof: representative cornering input can feather throttle below wheelie intent while sustained full throttle still triggers wheelies when car/track conditions allow.
- Tuning evidence: ramp windows, wheelie threshold/dwell/intent condition, and F2/debug visibility are recorded.
- Regression guard: existing boost, landing payoff, and beginner hold-up-to-go behavior remain intact.

title: Add cartoonish wheelie particles and sound cues
description:
Make wheelies more readable and more fun with lightweight host visual effects, phone cues, and audio.

Background:
The previous wheelie feedback bead added baseline readable feedback, but player difficulty suggests the state still needs stronger immediate communication. If steering authority drops, the game should clearly sell the wheelie as an intentional stunt state. The desired tone is arcade/cartoon: playful sparks, whoosh, stars, rubber chirps, or comic-style burst elements rather than realistic simulation only.

Scope:
- Add pooled/cheap particle effects for wheelie entry and sustain: e.g. rear-wheel dust/sparks, small neon skid streaks, star pops, or comic burst puffs.
- Add distinct sound cues: short wheelie entry chirp/whoop, sustained engine strain color if not already clear, clean landing punch, bad landing thunk/wobble.
- Make effects state-driven from existing vehicle handling/stunt state rather than per-frame logs or ad-hoc polling.
- Add reduced-motion and performance fallbacks: lower particle count, disable optional screen shake, keep audio short.
- Keep Local architecture intact: host renders world effects; phones only show light HUD/haptics. Remote viewers may render their own lightweight effects later but should degrade gracefully.
- Avoid obscuring name tags, race HUD, QR/lobby overlays, or controller controls.

Acceptance criteria:
- Spectators can identify wheelie entry/sustain/landing at a glance.
- A driver receives a distinct cue that steering authority changed because of wheelie state.
- Effects are visually playful but still readable in race and derby.
- No per-frame console logging.
- 4-player local smoke remains stable; effects use pooling or capped counts so host FPS is not meaningfully degraded.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/browser-light CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/wheelie-feedback-effects.test.js` covers state-driven effect triggers, pooling/capped counts, reduced-motion behavior, and no per-frame logging.
- Normal E2E/visual smoke: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/visual-effects.spec.ts tests/e2e/console-errors.spec.ts` proves wheelie entry/sustain/landing cues render without console errors and do not occlude HUD/name tags/control surfaces.
- Performance proof: 4-player Local smoke shows host FPS is not meaningfully degraded and particle/audio counts remain capped.
- Local architecture guard: host renders world effects; phones only show light HUD/haptics, with Remote viewer effects degrading independently later.

title: Add touch/controller regression tests without tanking CI performance
description:
Add focused test coverage for controller input shaping and touch behavior while keeping CI cost bounded.

Background:
Current E2E helpers drive players mostly through keyboard because touch simulation is complex. That leaves joystick dead zones, curves, touch cancellation, multitouch pedals, and shaped control packet behavior under-tested. We need coverage, but the fix must not make CI slow or flaky.

Scope:
- Add fast unit tests for ControlMapper axis shaping, keyboard ramping, throttle ramping, release behavior, and opposite-key neutralization.
- Add component-level or browser-light tests for the custom joystick: dead zone, full lock, touch cancel, and multitouch coexistence with pedals/fire.
- Add one small Playwright mobile-context smoke only where the browser event path matters; avoid a full 4-player WebGL flow unless necessary.
- Keep heavier playtest scripts local/manual or behind an opt-in script, not the default CI path.
- Use deterministic timers/fake clocks where possible instead of long waits.
- Ensure debug output is high signal and not per-frame gameplay logging.

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm test` remains fast enough for normal CI.
- New tests fail if joystick shaping or mapper semantics regress.
- At least one browser/mobile-context path proves touch input reaches shaped controls.
- Heavy visual/performance smoke remains separately runnable and documented.
- Test flake risk is explicitly minimized: no arbitrary multi-second sleeps where deterministic waits can be used.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal CI: `npm test` remains green and fast after adding focused mapper/joystick/throttle tests.
- Normal unit/browser-light CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/control-mapper.test.js tests/unit/joystick-shaping.test.js tests/unit/wheelie-intent.test.js` fails on regressions in dead zones, curves, keyboard ramping, throttle ramping, release behavior, opposite-key neutralization, and touch cancel.
- One mobile-context smoke: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/player-hud-state.spec.ts` or a focused controller-touch spec proves browser touch events reach shaped controls without running a full 4-player WebGL flow.
- Flake guard: no arbitrary multi-second sleeps where deterministic timers/hooks can be used; heavy visual/perf scripts remain opt-in and documented.

title: Add progressive steering authority and side-tilt recovery control
description:
Investigate and implement progressive steering behavior beyond simple full-authority vs wheelie-dampened steering, including useful control when the car is tilted, side-loaded, wall-loaded, recovering from a bad landing, or partly on its side.

Background:
Current physics already reduces steering at speed and heavily reduces authority during wheelie/airborne states. That is a good baseline, but the transition can still feel binary: grounded steering works, wheelie steering mostly disappears, and side/tilted recovery may feel like the player has no agency. A progressive model should communicate that steering authority follows wheel contact, tire load, roll angle, speed, wall contact, and stunt/recovery state rather than abruptly turning off.

Definition of the surrounding handling states:
- Grounded: normal tire steering, still reduced by speed so high-speed inputs do not spin out instantly. This is the full-authority reference state.
- Front-light: front wheels have partial contact or low load. Steering should soften before the car fully enters wheelie so the player feels the transition coming.
- Wheelie / rear-only: front tires cannot carve normally. Allow weak yaw/body influence so players can shape the stunt and line up a landing, but not take a normal racing line.
- Airborne: no tire steering. Optional air-control torque must be subtle, arcade-readable, and weaker than wheelie shaping.
- Side-tilted / two-wheel: when the car is on two wheels or partly on its side, steering/throttle/brake may apply a small readable yaw/roll/righting influence so recovery feels participatory rather than dead. This should be forgiving, not a magic instant reset.
- Wall contact / side-load: steering should help the car slide or peel away from walls where possible, not rail-lock into barriers. Coordinate with existing wall-slide grip behavior.
- Bad landing / recovery: keep the wobble/penalty readable, but ramp authority back in so the car does not snap from useless to full control.

Scope:
- Audit current steering authority inputs: speed, wheel contact, airborne/wheelie state, bad landing, wall contact, side-load, roll angle, and angular velocity.
- Introduce a single steering-authority calculation or clearly factored helper so the above states are readable and testable.
- Replace hard state cliffs where useful with progressive authority curves.
- Add optional side-tilt recovery control: when a car is on two wheels or partly on its side, steering/throttle/brake should contribute a small readable yaw/roll/righting influence if it feels fun and does not break physics.
- Keep front-wheel steering physically plausible: no precise tire steering while fully airborne; any air/tilt control should be weaker and arcade-readable.
- Expose tuning values in config/F2 where practical.
- Add telemetry so playtesters can see current steering authority, the dominant limiter, and why input is reduced.

Acceptance criteria:
- Steering authority changes feel gradual across grounded, front-light, wheelie, airborne, wall-loaded, bad landing, and side-tilted states.
- Side-tilted cars give players a limited but understandable recovery input instead of feeling completely dead.
- Wall contact and side-load behavior does not make cars stick harder to barriers.
- Wheelie tradeoff remains meaningful: players cannot carve tight corners while front wheels are up.
- Behavior is covered by state-level tests for authority calculation and at least one side-tilt or bad-landing recovery scenario.
- Tuning does not introduce frequent flips, spin-outs, or host performance regressions.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/steering-authority.test.js` covers grounded, front-light, wheelie/rear-only, airborne, side-tilted, wall-loaded, bad-landing, speed, contact, roll angle, and dominant limiter outputs.
- Integration/sim proof: at least one side-tilt or bad-landing recovery scenario gives limited player agency without instant magic reset, and wall contact does not increase barrier sticking.
- Tuning evidence: F2/debug overlay shows current steering authority, dominant limiter, and relevant tuning values.
- Gameplay guard: wheelie tradeoff remains meaningful; fully airborne cars do not get precise tire steering, and new recovery forces do not create frequent flips/spin-outs or host perf regressions.

title: Smooth derby bowl flat->wall transition (one revolved profile -> mesh + trimesh; fix inverted concavity)
description:
BUG/QUALITY: The derby "bowl" arena is undercooked — the flat floor -> curved wall join is a hard crease that catches/stops cars, and the floor is actually an inverted DOME, not a bowl.

ROOT CAUSE (verified): the bowl is FOUR disjoint pieces that don't agree at the rim:
- Visual floor: concave-ish CircleGeometry, `setZ(-height)`, `height=(r/R)^2*concavity*R` (`TrackFactory.js:268-305`, `:285`) — the sign makes it a raised-centre DOME.
- Visual wall: separate open CylinderGeometry from y=0..height (`TrackFactory.js:664-712`).
- Physics floor: a FLAT cuboid at y~=0.1 (`PhysicsSystem.js:171-189`, used for type 'bowl' at `GameHost.js:612-616`) — the concave mesh has NO matching collider.
- Physics wall: 64 outward-leaning boxes, base at y~=0 (`PhysicsSystem.js:516-555`).
After `rotation.x=-pi/2` the floor rim sits ~`concavity*R` (~4u) BELOW the wall base at y=0 -> a vertical lip + an unblended ~60 degree dihedral the wheel raycasts must climb instantly.

FIX: ONE radial profile `bowlProfile(r)` with a C1 (tangent-continuous) circular fillet joining floor->arc->wall, revolved into a single vertex grid feeding BOTH the visual BufferGeometry AND a Rapier trimesh collider — copy the dunes precedent (`terrain.js:28-85` + `PhysicsSystem.js:198-222`) which already does one-grid->mesh+physics correctly. Remove the flat-ground-box + `_createBowlWallBarrier` path for bowls. Fix the inverted concavity sign. Expose `filletRadius`/`floorConcavity` as data + F2/debug knob. Coexists with FB-derbywall (that owns the moving shrink boundary; this owns the static join).

TESTS: tangent-continuity invariant at the floor->wall seam (no normal discontinuity > threshold); cross-section debug-render; sim: a car driven at the rim rolls up the wall without stopping dead (speed-retention gate); derby-bowl still validates (no flat box, trimesh present).

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/gaps/derby-map-reliability.md §1. Conceptual dep on planning bead FB-geoframe (not yet cut as br bead).
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/bowl-profile.test.js tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` proves C1 tangent continuity at the floor-to-wall seam, no inverted dome sign, visual mesh and Rapier trimesh are generated from the same radial profile, and the flat ground box path is not used for bowls.
- Sim proof: a car driven from flat floor up the rim retains speed within the agreed threshold and does not catch/dead-stop at the seam.
- Debug artifact: cross-section render or diagnostics show floor, fillet, wall, normals, collider source, filletRadius, and floorConcavity.
- Scope guard: this bead owns the static bowl join; the moving shrink wall/collider remains owned by `br-fb-derbywall-shrink-7r5`.

title: Checkpoints are axis-aligned + ignore Y -> missed/false triggers on curves & airborne
description:
BUG: Race checkpoints use axis-aligned boxes with no orientation and ignore height (Y), so on curved procedural tracks cars miss/false-trigger them, and airborne or under-track cars trigger them.

ROOT CAUSE (verified): checkpoints are AABBs — `Track.js:204-216` ("Simple box check"), `:215` returns `dx < halfWidth && dz < halfWidth`. Procedural checkpoints store NO tangent (`ProceduralTrackGenerator.js:206`). So: (a) on a curve the box faces world axes, not track flow -> a car crossing at an angle can miss it or trigger the wrong one; (b) `:204` accepts `{x,y,z}` but only tests X/Z -> a car flying over (or below) the checkpoint still triggers it.

FIX:
- Store each checkpoint's TANGENT (track-flow direction) at generation (`ProceduralTrackGenerator` + oval/derby configs).
- Test crossing against an ORIENTED gate: a line-segment crossing perpendicular to the tangent (did the car pass through the gate plane this frame), not an AABB.
- Add a Y/height band so airborne/under-track cars do not count.

TESTS (§12.3 / sim): a bot following the racing line crosses every checkpoint IN ORDER on several curvy seeds (no misses); a car driven airborne over a checkpoint does NOT trigger it; oval still works.

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/feedback-design-pass.md §8.3 / §2 (#4).
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/checkpoint-gates.test.js tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` proves generated checkpoints store tangent/height bands and oriented gate crossing works on curvy seeds.
- Race sim proof: a bot following the racing line crosses every checkpoint in order across several curved procedural seeds with no misses/false positives.
- Airborne regression: a vehicle above or below the gate height band does not trigger the checkpoint; oval/simple tracks continue to pass.
- Scope guard: stable seat id may be used only as display tiebreaker, not to hide geometry/race-order uncertainty.

title: Curb collider rotated by radial angle not tangent -> inverted corners / curbs catch cars
description:
BUG: Curb/edge colliders are rotated wrong, producing "inverted corners" and curbs that catch/stop cars. Single highest-ROI map fix (touches To-Do items 8 "fix curb", 27 "inverted corners", and contributes to 28a "cars stop at walls").

ROOT CAUSE (verified): `_createCircularBarrier` (`PhysicsSystem.js:429-450`) places 48 box colliders around the circle but rotates EACH by the radial angle theta — `setRotation(_eulerToQuat(0, angle, 0))` at `:443` — while the code's OWN comment at `:438` says the tangent is `theta + pi/2`. So each curb box's long axis points radially (outward), not tangentially (around the circle): the ring becomes a jagged cog, not a smooth band -> "curbs not circular" + cars catching on the facets.

FIX:
- Derive the curb-box yaw from the TANGENT vector: `atan2(-tangent.z, tangent.x)` (matches this file's negative-Z yaw convention). Do NOT use a blind `angle + pi/2` — it is convention-fragile.
- Unify with `_createEdgeBarrier` so both read orientation from one tangent/normal source (ties to planning bead FB-geoframe, §8.0).

NOT A BUG (do not "fix"): the spline edge-barrier yaw `atan2(-dz, dx)` at `PhysicsSystem.js:378` was investigated and is CORRECT for this convention (matches the start-line at `TrackFactory.js:503`). Keep only a coverage test there.

TESTS (§12.3 geometry invariant): assert each curb collider's long-axis is PERPENDICULAR to its radius (`longAxis . radius ~= 0`) — this FAILS on current code and PASSES after the fix; assert edge-barrier normals point to the drivable side per-edge.

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/feedback-design-pass.md §8.0/§8.1.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` includes a failing-before/passing-after invariant that each circular curb collider long axis is perpendicular to its radius and edge-barrier normals point to the drivable side.
- Sim proof: a representative car can graze the circular curb at multiple angles without inverted-corner snagging or sudden stop beyond the configured collision-loss threshold.
- Scope guard: do not change the already-correct spline edge-barrier yaw except to add coverage; circular curb yaw must derive from tangent/normal convention rather than a blind angle offset.

title: Derby shrink wall: mesh scaled but collider not, broken glow, dead API, dt-less push
description:
BUG: The derby SHRINKING arena wall is visually/physically desynced, its glow update is broken, it has dead API, and its inward push is frame-rate dependent. (Distinct from the static bowl-transition bug br-fb-bowltransition-3ij — this is the moving shrink boundary.)

ROOT CAUSE (verified):
1. `DerbySystem` scales the wall MESH (`this.wallMesh.scale.set(scale,1,scale)`, `DerbySystem.js:381`) but the physics collider is built ONCE in PhysicsSystem and never resized -> cars hit an invisible old wall or phase through the visible shrunk one.
2. The scaled object is `track.barriers[0]` (`GameHost.js:911`), which TrackFactory builds as a `THREE.Group` (`TrackFactory.js:611,665`), NOT a mesh — so the warning-glow material update (`DerbySystem.js:384`) traverses the wrong type and is unreliable.
3. `DerbySystem.setWallCollider()` (`:187`) is DEAD API, never wired.
4. `_pushVehiclesInward` applies a fixed `pushForce = 10` impulse every update WITHOUT dt scaling (`:397, :399, :418-421`) -> gameplay changes with client frame rate (violates the CLAUDE.md frame-rate-independence rule).

FIX:
- Drive BOTH mesh and collider from one `arenaRadius(t)` source; rebuild/resize the Rapier collider in lockstep with the visual scale at each shrink step (no gap).
- Fix the glow update to traverse the Group's meshes (or restructure so it targets a known mesh).
- dt-scale the inward push impulse.
- Wire `setWallCollider` into the lockstep, or delete it.

TESTS: sim asserts a car at the visual wall radius is in collider contact (no gap) at several shrink stages; inward push produces the same displacement at 30 vs 60 fps (frame-rate independent).

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/feedback-design-pass.md §8.2 / §2 (#3, #7).
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/derby-wall-shrink.test.js tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` proves visual wall radius and Rapier collider radius stay synchronized at several shrink stages, glow traversal targets actual meshes, and dead `setWallCollider` API is either wired or removed.
- Frame-rate proof: inward push produces equivalent displacement at 30/60/120 fps within tolerance because force/impulse is dt-scaled.
- Sim proof: a car at the visible wall radius contacts the collider without invisible old-wall gap or phase-through at early/mid/late shrink.
- Scope guard: this bead owns moving shrink boundary behavior only; static bowl floor-to-wall geometry remains owned by `br-fb-bowltransition-3ij`.

title: Every gameplay mode x track must spawn a valid host-rendered map
description:
BUG: Several gameplay modes can spawn a broken/empty host-rendered map — players drop into a wall-less, surface-less void. This is a silent error path, not a one-off. This is about gameplay mode × track on the host renderer; Local phones remain controllers, and Remote viewer validation is separate/degrading.

OWNER DECISION (2026-06-29): invalid arenas should be impossible for valid shipped specs/seeds. Do not implement fallback-to-another-map as a normal recovery path. Runtime validation is a fail-loud tripwire: block the start, show a visible error, emit telemetry/logging, dump debug evidence, and never enter the spawn loop.

ROOT CAUSE (verified against code):
- `GameHost.js:635-637` SILENTLY swallows track-build errors (logs + continues). `_createTrack` nulls `this.track` during cleanup (`:594`); on a build throw (e.g. bad id -> `loadTrack` 404) the next line derefs the null `this.track` in `getSpawnPosition` (`:876`).
- Quiet half-builds: `_createTrackSurface` returns null for unknown `geometry.type` (`TrackFactory.js:143`); `_createSplineTrack` returns null on bad edges (`:377`); barriers can be empty (`PhysicsSystem.js:172,263`). In these cases the track object can still build, the flat ground box can still get created (`GameHost.js:613`), and cars spawn onto a void.
- Secondary: `_resolveTrackId` (`GameHost.js:959-974`, `:964`) passes unknown ids through and random arena pick uses `Math.random()`.

FIX:
1. Prove valid map construction before runtime: schema/generator/unit/integration validators must prove shipped specs/seeds cannot produce invalid maps. A generated map that fails validation is a generator bug and a test failure, not a runtime branch.
2. Add `validateArena(track, gameplayMode, playerCount)` after build + collider creation (after `setPhysicsBodies`, `GameHost.js:620`), BEFORE any car spawns: assert geometry built, colliders present, derby walls closed / race checkpoints ordered, every spawn in-bounds and ON GROUND via downward raycast.
3. Spawn safety is part of validity: enough spawns for N, no modulo reuse, no pairwise overlap, car-footprint clearance from walls/voids, no initial collider penetration, sane heading. This bead depends on `br-fb-spawncap-qi9`.
4. Replace the silent catch with fail-loud blocked start: emit structured `map:invalid`, preserve/return to a safe lobby state, show a visible host error, capture logs/telemetry, dump debug artifacts, and DO NOT call `this.track.getSpawnPosition` or start the round.
5. Tighten `_resolveTrackId`: concrete non-`random` selections either build the requested concrete id or fail loud if incompatible; unknown ids are rejected/fail-loud before load; `random` records requested selector and resolved concrete arena. No hidden substitution.
6. Telemetry/debug schema: `{mode, requestedTrackId, resolvedTrackId, seed, playerCount, reasons[], exceptionHash, stack, validatorVersion, debugArtifactPaths[], stayedInLobby}`. Tests assert this payload, not only visible text.

TESTS:
- Unit/integration normal CI: exhaustive valid gameplay-mode × track matrix (procedural, oval, derby-bowl/arena/coliseum/dunes, random SEEDED/forced through concrete arenas), plus invalid id/config/loader-404/bad JSON cases. Assert specific failure reasons for injected breakage (null surface, empty barriers, wall gap, off-ground spawn, <3 race checkpoints, overlap/penetration).
- Playwright normal CI: one small host-path gate through `?testMode=1` / `window.__jjTestResult`: representative race + derby valid starts, and one invalid start that stays in lobby/blocked-start state with telemetry/debug evidence and no spawn loop.
- Full browser mode×track matrix is opt-in/nightly unless it proves fast and stable. It should save debug PNGs as artifacts on failure, not depend on flaky pixel comparisons.

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/gaps/derby-map-reliability.md §2. Depends on `br-fb-spawncap-qi9`; conceptual deps on planning beads FB-validator/FB-seed/FB-geoframe/FB-bowltransition until those are cut into br edges.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/map-validator.test.js tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` exhaustively validates shipped gameplay-mode x track specs, seeded random/concrete arenas, invalid id/config/loader-404/bad JSON cases, geometry/collider presence, closed derby walls, ordered race checkpoints, spawn ground/raycast/overlap/penetration/heading, and structured failure reasons.
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/track-loading.spec.ts tests/e2e/full-game.spec.ts` includes one representative valid race start, one representative valid derby start, and one invalid start that stays in lobby/blocked-start state with visible error and telemetry/debug evidence.
- Fail-loud proof: no normal path silently falls back to another concrete map id; concrete selections build the requested id or block start, while random records requested selector plus resolved id/seed.
- Evidence artifact: invalid-map diagnostics include mode, requestedTrackId, resolvedTrackId, seed, playerCount, reasons, exceptionHash, validatorVersion, debugArtifactPaths, and stayedInLobby.

title: In-progress join QR overlay covers HUD; redundant QR in lobby
description:
BUG/UX: The in-progress join QR overlay can cover the HUD, and the lobby shows a redundant QR (To-Do item 11 "hide in-progress join QR code when in lobby"; the QR also overlaps the lap counter).

ROOT CAUSE (verified): the persistent room-code QR overlay is `position:fixed` bottom-left at `z-index:1000` (`RoomCodeOverlayUI.js:74-85`), above the Race HUD (`z-index:50`), so it can cover the lap counter/timer during racing; meanwhile the lobby renders its own large QR too, so both can show at once.

FIX: a QR visibility STATE MACHINE driven by game state:
- Lobby/waiting: show the large lobby QR only; hide the persistent overlay.
- Racing: show the small persistent overlay only, minimized, repositioned OUT of the lap-counter/HUD zone, with z-index BELOW the HUD.
- Never both at once.

TESTS (E2E): overlay is hidden in lobby; during racing the overlay does not overlap the HUD element bounds and sits below it in z-order.

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/feedback-design-pass.md §9 (FB-qr) / §2 (#6).
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/join-flow.spec.ts tests/e2e/bugfix-regressions.spec.ts` proves lobby shows only the large lobby QR, active race shows only the small persistent QR, and both never render simultaneously.
- Layout proof: during racing, QR overlay bounding box does not overlap lap/timer/HUD bounds and its z-index is below gameplay HUD where required.
- Regression proof: late join QR/code still works from racing state after repositioning/minimizing.

title: Silent 16-spawn cap: player 17 spawns on top of player 1 (blocks no-caps)
description:
BUG: A silent spawn cap breaks the "no player caps" intent — player 17 spawns ON TOP of player 1.

ROOT CAUSE (verified): `Track.getSpawnPosition` does `spawnPositions[playerIndex % spawnPositions.length]` (`Track.js:153`), and the procedural generator emits a FIXED grid of `gridRows = 8` x 2 = 16 spawns (`ProceduralTrackGenerator.js:170`). So beyond 16 players the modulo wraps and players spawn stacked on each other. There is no server cap; this CLIENT cap is the actual blocker to the owner's "let 60 players in, no caps" requirement.

FIX:
- Add `generateSpawns(track, N) -> Spawn[N]`: produce N guaranteed-length, NON-OVERLAPPING spawn poses for arbitrary N, for EVERY track and derby arena (extend grid rows / spiral outward / pack within bounds, on-ground, facing track direction).
- Remove the `% length` wraparound in `getSpawnPosition`; index directly into the N-length set.
- Spawns must stay in-bounds and on the drivable surface (raycast down) for the chosen arena.

TESTS: generator returns N non-overlapping spawns (no two within a car diameter) for N up to e.g. 64 across every track/arena; soak: 32+ Playwright controllers each get a distinct spawn; integrates with the no-cap input work (FB-keys / input-expansion).

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/gaps/input-expansion.md (no-cap finding) + docs/plans/feedback-design-pass.md §6.3.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/spawn-generation.test.js tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` proves `generateSpawns(track, N) -> Spawn[N]` returns guaranteed-length non-overlapping, on-ground, in-bounds, sane-heading spawns for every shipped race track and derby arena up to at least N=64.
- Regression proof: `Track.getSpawnPosition` no longer modulo-wraps; requesting player 17 cannot reuse player 1's spawn unless the caller explicitly passes a validated generated spawn set with unique ids.
- Normal E2E or opt-in soak: `JJ_SOAK_PLAYERS=32 npx playwright test tests/e2e/around-couch-soak.spec.ts` proves 32 controllers get distinct spawns with no immediate overlap/penetration.
- Evidence artifact: spawn diagnostics report min pair distance, ground raycast success, wall/void clearance, heading, track/ruleset, seed, and any rejected candidates.

title: Startup loading spinner never auto-dismisses / no skip
description:
BUG/UX: The startup loading spinner never auto-dismisses and has no skip — if engine init stalls it shows forever, and even normally it is an indefinite spinner (To-Do item 10 "fix spinners on start, skip them").

ROOT CAUSE (verified): `#loading-overlay` + `.loading-spinner` (`frontend/host/index.html:243-246`, CSS `:59-74`) is hidden only by a JS call on successful init; there is no timeout, no skip control, and no progress — if init throws or hangs the overlay persists.

FIX:
- Add a hard timeout that auto-dismisses (or shows an error+retry) after N seconds.
- Add a visible "Skip" affordance.
- Only show the overlay if init exceeds ~X ms (preload/caching already shortened startup), and show progress rather than an infinite spinner.

TESTS (E2E): overlay auto-hides within a bound on normal start; Skip dismisses it; a simulated init stall shows the timeout/error path, never an infinite spinner.

Full design + file:line: docs/plans/feedback-design-pass.md §9 (FB-spin) / §2 (#5).
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/bugfix-regressions.spec.ts tests/e2e/console-errors.spec.ts` or a focused loading-overlay spec proves normal startup hides the overlay within a bound, Skip dismisses it, and a simulated init stall shows retry/error rather than an infinite spinner.
- Accessibility/UX proof: skip/retry controls are keyboard reachable and visible without covering critical join/game controls.
- Scope guard: no per-frame logs or arbitrary multi-second sleeps in tests; use deterministic init hooks or bounded waits.

title: Observability and analytics integration for Joystick Jammers
description:
Build the game-side observability and analytics layer for Joystick Jammers, using PostHog as the product/gameplay/error correlation plane and Grafana as the infrastructure/service-health plane.

Intent:
- Understand how people actually play: join funnels, controller confidence, first input, reconnects, starts, match completion, wheelies, boosts, resets, map reliability, weapons, and churn/abandonment points.
- Debug production issues with correlated client/server context: release, room analytics id, match id, anonymous player id, role, mode, track, seed, device/browser, and error fingerprint.
- Keep complexity under control: do not introduce Loki or broad raw-log search in v1. Prefer structured first-party events and exceptions over dumping all logs.
- Reuse shared PPaS infrastructure: PostHog should be core shared infrastructure; Grafana should be centralized and separated from unrelated projects by labels/folders/datasources as appropriate.

Architecture stance:
- PostHog is the correlation plane for client events, server events, RUM-style samples, and client/server exception groups.
- Grafana is for infra/perf: host/container metrics, active rooms/players, Socket.IO event rate, disconnect rate, process health, and alerting.
- Domain events must be emitted through a tiny JJ telemetry wrapper so PostHog can be swapped, disabled in local/dev, sampled, or backed by a local/debug sink without scattering vendor calls through gameplay code.
- Privacy is part of the feature: no raw player names, no raw room codes, no raw IPs, no arbitrary free-text analytics properties, and no per-frame/per-tick event spam.

This lineage is intentionally blocked by a local placeholder bead representing external PPaS shared-infra work. If Beads gains real cross-project dependencies later, replace that placeholder with the actual PPaS dependency. Until then, close the blocker manually only when the shared PostHog + central Grafana decision/infrastructure is ready enough for game integration.
acceptance_criteria:
Epic acceptance before close:
- All child observability beads close with privacy, no-spam, correlation, and rollback/disable evidence.
- No raw player names, room codes, IPs, free-text payloads, or per-frame/per-tick analytics are emitted in normal gameplay.
- Telemetry can be disabled or routed to a safe local/debug sink without gameplay failures.
- Dashboards and operations docs identify release, room analytics id, match id, anonymous player id, role, topology/ruleset, track, seed, device/browser, and error fingerprint where allowed by the privacy model.

title: External blocker: PPaS shared PostHog and central Grafana stack
description:
This is a local placeholder blocker for work that belongs in the PPaS/shared-infra project, not in this game repo.

Why it exists:
- The desired architecture is PostHog as core shared infrastructure plus centralized Grafana for infra/service metrics.
- The game-side telemetry beads should not bake in final hostnames, auth rules, tokens, retention policies, or dashboard provisioning until the shared PPaS observability stack is ready.
- Cross-project Beads dependencies may not be supported here, so this bead represents the external dependency inside the Joystick Jammers graph.

External outcome needed before closing this blocker:
- A PPaS-owned PostHog endpoint exists or the team has explicitly chosen the hosted/self-hosted path, with project/API key strategy, ingestion hostname, admin auth, retention policy, and source-map upload path documented.
- A centralized Grafana path exists, either reusing the Triton Grafana stack or a new PPaS-managed stack, with project labels/folders for Joystick Jammers separated from Facemorph and other services.
- A decision exists on whether the game publishes directly to these services from production, through a small PPaS collector/proxy, or through env-configured direct sinks.
- Required secrets/env names are known: PostHog host/key, optional error tracking flags, Grafana/Prometheus scrape endpoint config, and any Cloudflare Access/WAF requirements.

Close this bead manually when the PPaS infra is ready enough for game-side integration. If real cross-project dependency support is added, replace this placeholder with a dependency on the PPaS bead and close/remove this local stand-in.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- External/manual evidence: PPaS has a documented PostHog decision and endpoint/project/key strategy, plus a central Grafana path with project separation for Joystick Jammers.
- Required integration contract exists: ingestion hostnames, env var names, source-map upload path, retention policy, admin/auth model, collector/proxy/direct-sink decision, and any Cloudflare Access/WAF requirements.
- Game-side safety guard: do not close this local placeholder merely because game code can use a local debug sink; close only when external shared infra is ready enough for the dependent telemetry contract bead.
- If cross-project Beads dependencies become available, replace this placeholder with the real PPaS bead reference and record that migration before closing/removing it.

title: Create centralized Grafana views for Joystick Jammers infra and service health
description:
Add Joystick Jammers to the centralized Grafana stack without mixing it into Facemorph or unrelated service views.

Scope:
- Define labels/folders/datasource strategy for `project=joystick-jammers`, `service=game-server`, `env=prod|staging`.
- Add Grafana panels for process/container health, CPU/RAM/disk, container restarts, active rooms, active players, disconnect rate, exception rate, Socket.IO event rate, failed join/reconnect counters, and report volume.
- Add alert candidates: app down, exception spike, disconnect spike, scrape missing, disk space low, container restart loop.
- Add links from Grafana panels to relevant PostHog dashboards filtered by time range/release/env where practical.
- Keep Grafana operational and low-cardinality; do not mirror every product/gameplay event here.

Acceptance criteria:
- Grafana shows game service health separately from Facemorph.
- Operators can jump from an infra spike to PostHog for release/session/event context.
- Dashboard provisioning/runbook is documented enough for PPaS/shared-infra maintainers.

title: Validate telemetry privacy, correlation, and no-spam behavior end to end
description:
Add the test and validation layer for the observability work. This is the gate that prevents telemetry from becoming noisy, leaky, or uncorrelatable.

Scope:
- Unit tests for telemetry schema validation, redaction, required metadata, allowlisted event names, size caps, sampling, and disabled/no-op behavior.
- Server tests for structured event capture, exception capture, metrics endpoint output, and low-cardinality labels.
- E2E tests or browser integration tests for app boot, join funnel, first input, one client exception, and one reconnect path with telemetry captured through a test sink.
- Privacy tests: prove raw room code, player name, query string, raw IP, long tokens, and arbitrary text are not emitted as analytics properties or metric labels.
- No-spam tests: prove high-rate control updates and gameplay loops do not emit per-frame/per-packet events.
- Correlation test: a synthetic room/join/start/error path produces client and server events that can be joined by release + roomAnalyticsId + matchId.

Acceptance criteria:
- `npm test`/server tests include focused telemetry coverage without requiring live PostHog/Grafana credentials.
- A test sink can assert event payloads deterministically.
- Documentation explains how to run an opt-in live smoke against the real shared infra after PPaS is ready.

title: Document observability operations, retention, and incident workflow
description:
Document how this observability layer is operated after it ships, so it stays useful and does not become an unowned pile of dashboards.

Scope:
- Document env vars/secrets needed by the game: PostHog host/key, telemetry enable flags, source-map upload token/path if applicable, metrics scrape exposure, env/release labels.
- Document retention expectations: sampled session replay, event retention, error retention, metrics retention, and how to lower volume if the game grows.
- Document incident workflow: start in Grafana for infra/service symptoms, jump to PostHog for release/session/event/error context, then file or link a Beads bug.
- Document privacy posture for analytics: what is collected, what is deliberately not collected, and how local/dev disables telemetry.
- Document when to add Loki later: only if structured events plus Docker logs are insufficient and raw searchable logs are repeatedly needed.

Acceptance criteria:
- README/docs include a concise operator path for debugging production issues.
- The runbook distinguishes game-side responsibilities from PPaS/shared-infra responsibilities.
- A future agent can rotate keys, disable telemetry, or investigate an incident without rediscovering the architecture.

title: Define JJ telemetry contract, privacy model, and correlation IDs
description:
Create the game-side telemetry contract before wiring any vendor SDK. This is the schema and policy that makes PostHog useful as a correlation plane while keeping player privacy and event volume sane.

Scope:
- Define a `TelemetryEvent` shape for client and server events: eventName, timestamp, release/build, env, role, source, roomAnalyticsId, matchId, playerAnalyticsId, mode, trackId, mapSeed, deviceClass, browserFamily, and bounded properties.
- Define stable event naming conventions for product/gameplay, errors, server events, and perf samples.
- Define correlation IDs:
  - `roomAnalyticsId`: server-generated opaque id; never the raw room code.
  - `matchId`: generated at game start and attached to all match events.
  - `playerAnalyticsId`: anonymous browser/device/session id; never player display name.
  - `release`: git SHA or image tag shared by client and server.
- Define privacy rules: no raw player names, no raw room code in analytics, no raw IPs, no unbounded user text, no token/query-string capture, no arbitrary console dump as event properties.
- Define sampling rules: no per-frame/per-tick events; low-rate performance samples only; high-frequency gameplay state must be aggregated before emit.
- Define local/dev behavior: analytics disabled by default unless env-enabled, with a debug sink that logs sanitized events on demand.

Acceptance criteria:
- A schema/spec exists in the repo and is understandable without reading this bead.
- Every proposed downstream event maps to the schema and privacy policy.
- The contract explicitly distinguishes PostHog product/error events from Grafana metrics.
- Unit tests or lint-style validation cover allowlisted event names, required fields, property bounds, and privacy redaction.

title: Propagate release metadata and analytics IDs across host, controllers, and server
description:
Add the shared metadata needed to correlate client events, server events, errors, and Grafana metrics.

Scope:
- Inject client build metadata at Vite build time: git SHA/image tag and build timestamp.
- Expose server version metadata through a small endpoint such as `/version`, and include the same release/image tag in server telemetry and metrics.
- Generate `roomAnalyticsId` server-side when creating/reclaiming rooms and send it to the host and joined controllers without exposing it in public URLs.
- Generate `matchId` on each game start and propagate to host/controllers for all match-scoped events.
- Generate or load a privacy-safe anonymous `playerAnalyticsId` for controllers/viewers; keep it distinct from player name and Socket.IO sid.
- Include env/project labels consistently: `project=joystick-jammers`, `service=game-server|host-client|controller-client`, `env=prod|staging|local`.

Acceptance criteria:
- A host, controller, and server event from the same match can be joined by `roomAnalyticsId` + `matchId` + `release`.
- Existing room codes, player names, and raw socket IDs are not used as analytics identifiers.
- Tests cover metadata injection and propagation through create room, join, start game, reconnect, and return-to-lobby paths.

title: Implement client telemetry wrapper with PostHog sink and safe local fallback
description:
Implement a thin browser-side telemetry module that all host/controller code uses instead of importing PostHog directly throughout gameplay systems.

Scope:
- Add a small `TelemetryClient`/`telemetry` module with `init`, `capture`, `captureException`, `identifyAnonymous`, `setContext`, and `flush` style APIs.
- Support sinks:
  - PostHog sink when env-configured and enabled.
  - No-op sink for local/dev default.
  - Optional debug sink for tests/manual QA that records sanitized events in memory or prints one-time summaries.
- Enforce the telemetry contract at runtime: required metadata, allowlisted event names, property size caps, privacy redaction, and sampling.
- Ensure host and controller entry points initialize telemetry early enough to capture boot and initialization errors, but not so early that tests become flaky.
- Keep SDK loading bundled through npm/Vite if a dependency is added; no CDN imports.
- Respect reduced/no tracking local settings if introduced later.

Acceptance criteria:
- Host and player/controller pages can emit a sanitized `app_boot` and `route_view` event through the wrapper.
- When PostHog env is absent, the game runs normally and tests do not make network calls.
- Tests prove no raw player name, raw room code, query string, or oversized property is sent through the wrapper.
- The wrapper is the only browser code that talks to the vendor SDK directly.

title: Implement server telemetry, structured events, and exception capture
description:
Add structured server-side observability for Flask and Socket.IO without turning production into raw log search.

Scope:
- Add a server telemetry module with the same contract concepts as the browser wrapper.
- Capture structured server events for room lifecycle, joins, failed joins, reconnect attempts/results, host disconnects, game starts/ends, report submissions, and validation failure buckets.
- Capture server exceptions from Flask routes and Socket.IO handlers with release, env, handler name, roomAnalyticsId when available, and sanitized request/socket context.
- Add lightweight counters/histograms that Grafana can scrape: active rooms, active players, disconnected players, room creations, joins, failed joins, reconnect success/failure, Socket.IO event counts, exception counts, and handler latency buckets where practical.
- Avoid logging every `player_control_update`; aggregate or count high-volume paths instead.
- Keep raw IPs out of stored analytics; if needed, use salted hashes only for rate limits or dedupe.

Acceptance criteria:
- Server events and exceptions appear in the same PostHog project/correlation space as client events when enabled.
- `/health` remains lightweight, and any `/metrics` endpoint is safe for the central Grafana scrape path.
- Tests cover representative Socket.IO handler success/failure events and an exception path without leaking raw payloads.
- High-volume control updates do not emit per-packet analytics events.

title: Wire client and server error trace collection with source-map/release support
description:
Turn client and server exceptions into useful release-correlated error groups, with enough context to debug production issues without manual reproduction first.

Scope:
- Browser: capture `window.onerror`, `unhandledrejection`, WebGL context loss, Socket.IO connect errors, and initialization failures through the telemetry wrapper.
- Server: capture uncaught Flask/Socket.IO exceptions and explicit `captureException` calls through the server telemetry module.
- Add fingerprinting/throttling so one repeated failure does not spam PostHog or the report endpoint.
- Configure source-map generation/upload for Vite builds if PostHog error tracking is used, gated by env so local builds do not require credentials.
- Attach shared context: release, env, route, role, roomAnalyticsId, matchId, device/browser, mode, trackId/mapSeed when known.
- Link manual bug reports and auto-captured errors by fingerprint where practical, without putting raw screenshots or user text into analytics.

Acceptance criteria:
- A deliberate client exception in E2E/test mode produces one sanitized error event with release/context and no repeated spam.
- A deliberate server exception in a test route/handler produces one sanitized error event with handler/context.
- Source-map upload command/path is documented and CI/deploy-safe.
- Error capture can be disabled by env and does not break local/dev/test runs.

title: Add product and gameplay analytics events for play understanding
description:
Instrument the events that answer how people actually play Joystick Jammers and where they struggle.

Scope:
- Join/onboarding funnel: landing/host route, room created, player join started, join failed, player joined, first input, tutorial viewed/skipped/completed where available, game started.
- Controller confidence: controller connected/disconnected, reconnect attempted/succeeded/failed, visibility/background release behavior, input stalled, first throttle/steer/fire, input scheme once ControlMapper lands.
- Match flow: mode selected, race/derby started, match ended, return to lobby, play again, player count, duration buckets.
- Gameplay feel: wheelie entered/sustained/landed, bad landing, boost used, car reset requested, out-of-bounds reset, wall recovery, weapon pickup/fire/hit where bounded.
- Map reliability: track/arena id, seed, spawn count, map validation failure, reset/oob clusters by map.
- Keep event properties bounded and aggregated; do not emit per-frame/per-tick state.

Acceptance criteria:
- PostHog can build join funnel, reconnect health, map reliability, and wheelie/boost outcome dashboards from these events.
- Tests or instrumentation checks prove high-frequency gameplay loops do not emit unbounded event streams.
- Events include correlation IDs from the telemetry contract and are safe under the privacy model.

title: Add RUM-style performance samples and Grafana service metrics
description:
Capture low-rate performance data for both product correlation in PostHog and operational health in centralized Grafana.

Scope:
- Browser RUM-style samples: FPS bucket, frame-time bucket where available, WebGL init/context loss, page visibility/backgrounding, network reconnect count, player count, mode, camera mode, device class/browser family.
- Server metrics endpoint for Grafana/Prometheus: active rooms, active players, disconnected players, Socket.IO event counters, failed join/reconnect counters, exception counts, report counts, process uptime, optional handler latency buckets.
- Keep Grafana metrics numeric and low-cardinality. Do not label metrics by raw room, player, seed, or error message.
- Use PostHog for rich product correlation and Grafana for infra/service trends. Do not duplicate every gameplay event into Prometheus.
- Ensure the metrics endpoint can be safely scraped by the PPaS/Triton Grafana path, ideally behind network or Cloudflare Access rules from the infra bead.

Acceptance criteria:
- Grafana can graph active rooms/players, disconnect rate, exception rate, process health, and Socket.IO event volume without needing Loki.
- PostHog can correlate low-FPS samples with reports/resets/reconnect failures by release/match/device class.
- Tests cover metric names, low-cardinality labels, and no raw room/player identifiers in metrics.

title: Create PostHog dashboards for cross-layer gameplay and error analysis
description:
Build the actual analysis surfaces that make the telemetry useful, with PostHog as the place where product, gameplay, client errors, and server events cross-pollinate.

Scope:
- Release health dashboard: client exceptions, server exceptions, affected sessions/rooms, top fingerprints, release breakdowns.
- Join funnel dashboard: landing/host route -> room created -> join started -> player joined -> first input -> game started.
- Reconnect health dashboard: disconnects, reconnect attempts, reconnect success/failure, browser/device breakdown.
- Performance vs play dashboard: low FPS samples vs resets, disconnects, reports, abandonment, player count, mode.
- Map reliability dashboard: track/arena/seed buckets, car resets, OOB resets, reports, map/spawn failures.
- Wheelie/boost/stunt dashboard: wheelie entries, bad landings, clean landings, boost payoff, resets after wheelie, retention/abandonment correlations.
- Document naming, ownership, and how to filter by release/time/env.

Acceptance criteria:
- Dashboards can answer the debugging questions without raw Loki logs.
- Dashboard definitions or setup notes are stored in repo docs or a PPaS config path, depending on infra decision.
- Each dashboard states the required event names/properties so future telemetry changes do not silently break it.

title: Map authoring tool for racing tracks and derby arenas
description:
Build a local/developer-facing map generation and creation tool for both Race and Demolition Derby that follows the shared debug-lab pattern: production rendering/geometry paths where practical, deterministic seed controls, visible validation overlays, parameter tuning for uncertain defaults, screenshot/export hooks, and machine-readable diagnostics.

Background:
Current map work is focused on reliability bugs: invalid maps can silently spawn broken worlds, derby bowl/wall geometry has physics mismatches, checkpoints/spawns need stronger validation, and the project wants 2-32 player couch play without hard caps. A map authoring tool should make those constraints visible while designers create or tune maps. It must not become a decorative JSON editor that can export broken maps.

Scope:
- Provide a local browser/dev tool or equivalent workflow for creating, loading, tuning, validating, and exporting map definitions compatible with static/assets/tracks/*.json and procedural seed/config paths.
- Race authoring: loop/spline/oval/procedural controls, racing line preview, checkpoint orientation/height bands, barriers/curbs, spawn positions, pickup zones, lap metadata, and validation overlays.
- Derby authoring: arena shape/profile, bowl/coliseum/dunes variants, closed walls, shrink boundary preview, spawn packing, weapon/pickup zones, hazards, and validation overlays.
- Visualize physics-relevant data: colliders, wall normals, checkpoint planes, spawn footprints, out-of-bounds zones, surface height/raycast hits, pickup areas, and validation failures.
- Include deterministic seed browsing and quick randomization, with export/import preserving seed, tuning profile, and config needed to reproduce a map.
- Use the arena/map validator from the reliability work so the tool cannot mark a map good if it would spawn cars into a broken/surface-less world.
- Export an evidence package: map JSON, seed, tuning hash, screenshot/top-down debug render, and validator report.

Acceptance criteria:
- A developer can create or tune at least one race map and one derby arena, export them, and load them through the normal host path.
- Race exports include valid checkpoints, spawns for high N, barriers, and pickup zones; derby exports include valid closed bounds/shrink metadata, spawns, and pickup zones.
- The validator catches broken surfaces, missing colliders, invalid spawns, bad checkpoints, unsafe derby bounds, and insufficient/high-overlap spawns from inside the tool.
- Tests cover JSON round-trip, deterministic seed replay, race validation, derby validation, 32/64 spawn diagnostics, and at least one browser/E2E smoke that loads an exported map into the host path.
- The tool follows the shared debug-lab pattern and safely renders labels/errors/diagnostics.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit/integration CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/map-authoring-roundtrip.test.js tests/unit/map-validator.test.js tests/integration/track-physics.test.ts` covers JSON round-trip, deterministic seed replay, race validation, derby validation, collider/surface/checkpoint/spawn/pickup diagnostics, and 32/64 spawn reports.
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/map-authoring.spec.ts tests/e2e/track-loading.spec.ts` proves the tool loads, canvas is nonblank, diagnostics/screenshot/export hooks work, and at least one exported race map and one exported derby arena load through the normal host path.
- Validator authority: the tool cannot mark or export a map as good when normal host validation would fail; invalid inputs fail loud with diagnostics rather than silently falling back to another map.
- Policy guard: the accepted default queues race finish_grace late joiners and derby combat late joiners for the next race/round, but the map tool still validates dynamic safe spawn candidates for early race late joins, respawns, reconnects, future/custom policies, and soak tests.
- Evidence bundle: map JSON, seed, tuning hash, screenshot/top-down debug render, validator report, and host-load proof.

title: Multiplayer modes (local + remote), invites, and design/asset alignment
description:
Umbrella epic for the next wave of Joystick Jammers work, in three streams:

1. PLAY MODES — formalize a Local mode (today's single-screen, one-host-renders behaviour) and add a Remote Play mode where physically-separated people each join from their own device into a shared room. Includes deeplink invites and rejoinable rooms.

2. DESIGN ALIGNMENT — bring every in-game surface (lobby, HUD, menus, results, phone controller) into line with the front-page neon aesthetic. Source of truth is the live landing page; see docs/design-brief.md.

3. ASSETS & USABILITY — car models, own-car identification, car customisation, pickup models, and assorted usability improvements.

See docs/design-brief.md for the design direction. Architecture decisions (e.g. host-authoritative rendering, viewer vs controller roles) are captured in the child beads.

title: Game-mode architecture: selectable Local vs Remote room sessions
description:
Introduce a first-class game-mode concept at the room/session layer so a room is explicitly Local or Remote, and refactor today's behaviour into 'Local mode' without changing its UX.

LOCAL MODE (current behaviour, formalized): one big screen acts as host and renders the shared world; phones are pure controllers. This is the default and must keep working exactly as today.

Why first: Remote Play, deeplink invites, and rejoinable rooms all branch on mode, so the abstraction has to exist before they can be built cleanly.

Scope:
- Add a mode field to room/session state (server + client).
- Mode is chosen at room creation and surfaced in the lobby UI.
- Extract current single-screen flow into the Local path; no behaviour change.
- Define the role vocabulary used everywhere downstream: HOST (renders authoritative sim), CONTROLLER (sends input for a car), VIEWER (watches a synced view, optionally also a controller).
- Tests: existing full-game E2E still passes under explicit Local mode.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal server/client CI: `npm test` plus `python -m unittest server.test_socket_routing` proves room/session state carries explicit Local/Remote topology without breaking the current Local path.
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/full-game.spec.ts tests/e2e/game-modes.spec.ts tests/e2e/join-flow.spec.ts` proves existing Local host + phone-controller UX still works with explicit Local topology.
- Contract proof: room/session vocabulary uses topology (`local`, `remote`, `mixed`) separately from ruleset (`race`, `derby`) and roles (`host`, `controller`, `viewer`, `spectator`).
- Regression guard: Local phones remain controllers/HUD only; host renders the full shared 3D world.

title: Usability improvements: learn-to-play and general UX polish
description:
Grab-bag of usability work to help new players learn and reduce friction, complementing the existing first-run tutorial.

Candidate scope (split into sub-beads as it firms up):
- Clearer in-controller hints for steering/accelerate/brake/fire on first use.
- Better empty/loading/error states across join + lobby (neon-styled, see design-alignment).
- Surface room code, invite link, and player roster prominently in lobby.
- Onboarding for the new modes: explain Local vs Remote when creating a room.
- Spectator/viewer affordances for Remote (clear 'you are watching' vs 'you are driving').
- Accessibility passes (focus order, reduced motion, tap targets).
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Split guard: if the scope remains broad, create child beads for first-run hints, empty/loading/error states, lobby roster/invite surfacing, Local-vs-Remote explainer, spectator/viewer copy, and accessibility instead of landing a grab-bag patch.
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/player-tutorial.spec.ts tests/e2e/join-flow.spec.ts tests/e2e/player-hud-state.spec.ts tests/e2e/console-errors.spec.ts` proves onboarding, join/lobby, controller terminal states, and basic error states remain usable.
- Accessibility evidence: focus order, tap target sizing, reduced motion, and text overflow are checked on mobile and desktop viewports.
- UX evidence: screenshots or short notes cover first-use controller hints, room code/invite/roster visibility, Local-vs-Remote explanation, and spectator/viewer/driver distinction.

title: Per-model game-readiness & balance
description: Run every vehicle/kart model through the generic game-model-prep pass (normalize, rig, collider/CoM, balance, destruct, color, visual QA) so each is good to play and net-fair balanced (Mario-Kart style). Plan: docs/plans/per-model-game-readiness-and-balance-2026-06-28.md. Generic skill: .claude/skills/game-model-prep/. Gate: vehicle-model-validation skill. Depends on the asset import (.7).
acceptance_criteria:
Before closing this epic or any per-model rollout work, the vehicle model pipeline must satisfy all of the following:

- Each model import uses `.claude/skills/game-model-prep/SKILL.md` and `.claude/skills/vehicle-model-validation/SKILL.md`; the completion note must include the Stage H PASS report and separate evidence for visual/material QA versus gameplay tuning.
- Material/texture/light acceptance is first-class: `paint` recolors cleanly, `tyre` stays neutral, glass/metal/lights remain readable, no embedded cameras/lights sneak into selectable vehicles, texture/material budgets are recorded, and headlights/tail lights are oriented and attached correctly.
- `/car-viewer` is the local debug/QA editor for inspecting raw and normalized vehicle assets. Per-model evidence must include `/car-viewer` diagnostics plus actual host/render-harness screenshots for default color and at least two player colors.
- The prep-manifest runner must incorporate `br-modes-remote-play-design-48a.11.38` before treating model Stage H as complete.
- The first two pilot vehicles are `br-modes-remote-play-design-48a.11.10` and `.11.11` unless the human coordinator explicitly changes the pair. All remaining per-model beads stay blocked behind `br-modes-remote-play-design-48a.11.39` until cdilga personally playtests those first two cars and explicitly approves broad rollout.
- Manual human approval cannot be substituted by screenshots, automated tests, an agent self-review, or a generic claim that the skill passed.

title: Promote /car-viewer debug editor into the vehicle QA tool
description:
Make the existing `frontend/car-viewer` debug editor a first-class vehicle QA and authoring support tool in the model-import plan. This bead is about preserving and hardening the useful editor that already exists, then wiring it into the evidence flow for per-model work.

Background:
The current `/car-viewer` tool already loads `/debug-cars/manifest.json`, classifies materials into body/wheel/glass/light/other groups, supports recolor modes, shows headlights/tail lights/underglow/name tag/number behavior, and includes rig/suspension preview controls. That is valuable planning infrastructure and should not be treated as a throwaway debug page.

Scope:
- Keep `/car-viewer` or its successor as a documented local QA/editor surface for vehicle imports.
- Ensure it can load both `static/debug-cars/*` sources and normalized selectable vehicle catalog entries, so agents can compare raw imports against production-ready output.
- Preserve controls for material classification, naive-vs-normalized recolor comparison, paint color swatches, wheel/rig preview, suspension bob/steer preview, headlights/tail lights, underglow, name tag, number/decal readability, wireframe, and multi-angle inspection.
- Add or document screenshot/export evidence from the tool: material role summary, texture/material counts, active vehicle id, color, light toggle state, and captured views.
- Make clear that this is a local QA/debug tool, not a player-facing car picker. The actual picker still uses sanitized catalog ids and appearance payloads.
- Use the editor findings to feed the vehicle-model-validation Stage H report rather than replacing the formal gate.

Acceptance criteria:
- The model-import plan names `/car-viewer` (or successor path) as the manual/debug QA tool and requires its evidence for per-model visual review.
- The tool can inspect a raw debug car and a normalized selectable vehicle entry without hand-editing source code.
- Screenshots or exported diagnostics show recolor separation, wheel/rig preview, lights/underglow/name/number state, and material classification results.
- The workflow documents how an agent uses the tool before closing a model bead and how failures become authoring fixes rather than ad-hoc runtime hacks.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/car-viewer-diagnostics.test.js tests/unit/safe-rendering.test.js` covers diagnostics schema, material classification, recolor separation metadata, and literal rendering of hostile model names/errors.
- Normal E2E: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/car-viewer.spec.ts tests/e2e/console-errors.spec.ts` proves raw debug-car and normalized catalog modes load, canvas is nonblank, screenshots/diagnostics hooks work, and no console errors occur.
- Evidence bundle: exported diagnostics include active vehicle id, raw/normalized source, material roles, texture/material counts, paint color, light/underglow/name/number state, and multi-angle screenshots.
- Scope guard: `/car-viewer` is a local QA tool and never a player-facing trust boundary; production picker continues to use sanitized catalog ids.

title: Vehicle material, texture, and light acceptance gate
description:
Add an explicit material/texture/light acceptance gate to the vehicle import pipeline so imported models do not merely load - they are readable, recolorable, lit correctly, and production-usable in the actual game.

Background:
The model-prep plan already requires Stage H validation, but recent work showed unresolved issues around texturing/coloring and vehicle lights. The gate needs to make these failures first-class: no single-color blobs, no baked-light texture problems that block recolor, no missing paint slot, no wheels/glass/tyres recolored accidentally, no embedded GLB lights/cameras, and no headlights/tail lights that point the wrong way or detach from the rig.

Scope:
- Define and enforce normalized material roles: `paint`, `tyre`, `glass`, `metal/trim`, `light`, and any allowed decals/number surfaces.
- Require production recolor behavior: changing player color affects only paint/approved decal surfaces while tyres remain neutral, glass stays readable, lights remain emissive, and numbers/name markers stay legible.
- Validate texture/material budgets: texture sizes, material count, draw-call implications, no surprise giant baked textures, no embedded cameras, no embedded runtime lights, and no per-car PointLights in the selectable GLB path.
- Validate lighting/readability in actual host-style rendering, not only a neutral model viewer: front/rear/side/top screenshots, at least two paint colors, lights on/off, underglow where applicable, and a dark/neon track lighting case.
- Verify headlights/tail lights are oriented correctly, ride with suspension/body transforms, do not float when the rig animates, and remain visible without becoming blinding.
- Feed both deterministic checks and visual review: glTF inspection output, `/car-viewer` diagnostics, headless render screenshots, and the vehicle-model-validation Stage H report.
- Failure mode must be authoring/normalization fixes, not runtime heuristic hacks that hide bad source assets.

Acceptance criteria:
- The prep-manifest runner records material-role mapping, texture/material counts, recolor evidence, and light evidence for each model.
- A model cannot pass if paint recolor also tints tyres/glass/lights, if `paint` resolves to nothing, if light materials are missing/wrongly oriented, or if embedded lights/cameras sneak into selectable vehicles.
- Evidence includes `/car-viewer` diagnostics plus actual host/render-harness screenshots for default color and at least two player colors.
- The vehicle-model-validation Stage H completion note must call out material role pass/fail, texture budget pass/fail, and light orientation/readability pass/fail separately from physics/balance.
- At least the first two imported cars receive manual human playtest review before the broad model rollout continues.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Normal unit CI: `npx vitest run tests/unit/vehicle-material-gate.test.js tests/unit/vehicle-light-gate.test.js` rejects missing paint, paint bleeding into tyres/glass/lights, embedded cameras/lights, over-budget textures/materials, wrong light orientation, and unresolved material roles.
- Normal E2E/visual QA: `npx playwright test tests/e2e/car-viewer.spec.ts` captures default color plus at least two player colors across front/rear/side/top and a dark/neon lighting preset.
- Stage H report: vehicle-model-validation output must list material role pass/fail, texture budget pass/fail, recolor pass/fail, light orientation/readability pass/fail, and host-style render pass/fail separately from physics/balance.
- Manual gate linkage: first two pilot imports cannot unlock broad rollout until `br-modes-remote-play-design-48a.11.39` records cdilga's playtest approval.

title: Manual human playtest gate after first two vehicle imports
description:
Manual coordinator gate: the first two imported/normalized vehicle models must be personally playtested by cdilga before the broader vehicle rollout continues. Agents must not close this bead based only on automated tests, screenshots, or their own judgment.

Default first-two pair for graph enforcement:
- `br-modes-remote-play-design-48a.11.10` hatchback-sports
- `br-modes-remote-play-design-48a.11.11` sedan-sports

If the human coordinator chooses a different first-two pair before work starts, update this bead dependencies and description to match that explicit choice. Until then, the graph treats those two starter cars as the pilot imports.

Scope of the human playtest:
- Review each of the two cars in the car selection/customization path and the actual host game render.
- Try at least one race and one derby context if both are available at that point, or explicitly record which mode was unavailable.
- Check the subjective parts automation can miss: does the car look good, does recolor preserve readable materials, do lights/readability feel right, does the silhouette match the intended archetype, does the vehicle feel usable rather than merely passing physics metrics.
- Confirm the vehicle-model-validation Stage H evidence includes material/texture/light results, `/car-viewer` evidence, host screenshots, and relevant test/check output.

Acceptance criteria:
- This bead can close only after cdilga has actually playtested the first two cars at that time and posted explicit approval in Beads or Agent Mail.
- The approval must identify the two vehicle ids reviewed and state PASS for broad rollout, or list blockers/fixes.
- If either car fails the human review, this gate stays open and the failed model bead(s) get reopened or receive follow-up beads.
- No remaining per-model import/balance beads should be treated as ready for broad rollout until this manual gate is closed.
acceptance_criteria:
Evidence required before close:
- Manual only: cdilga must personally playtest the two pilot vehicle ids at that time; agents cannot close this from automated checks, screenshots, or proxy judgment.
- Required human note in Beads or Agent Mail: identifies the two vehicle ids, confirms race and derby tried or states which mode was unavailable, and says either PASS for broad rollout or lists blockers/fixes.
- Required technical evidence before human review: Stage H reports for both pilot cars include material/texture/light results, `/car-viewer` diagnostics, host/render screenshots, and relevant unit/E2E/check output.
- Failure behavior: if either car fails, this gate stays open and the failed per-model bead receives blockers or follow-up beads; no later per-model rollout bead should be treated as unblocked.
